behaviors.description: Conversation history passed to the LLM is trimmed to the most recent 10 messages when the full history exceeds the context window
behaviors.justification: This is a pure data-transformation task: the system takes a history list and truncates it before passing to the LLM. The failure (passing all N messages instead of trimming to 10) is catchable with a mocked LLM call by inspecting the payload—no need for real I/O or LLM behavior. Mocking the LLM does not hide the failure because the invariant is about what data *enters* the LLM, not what it produces.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the LLM call and verify that when conversation history size exceeds the context window, only the 10 most recent messages are passed to the LLM. Parameterize with history sizes of 5, 10, 15, and 50 messages, and verify the correct slice is selected in each case.

behaviors.description: The generated response addresses the customer's question rather than producing a generic or off-topic reply
behaviors.justification: Whether a response 'addresses the question' fundamentally depends on what the real LLM produces. Mocking the LLM means you control the output (e.g., mock it to return 'hello'), which hides the real failure: the LLM generating off-topic text. This is a non-deterministic quality metric that varies across LLM runs and requires the real model to test. You cannot catch LLM-quality failures without running the actual LLM.
behaviors.test_description: Call the real LLM with deterministic, well-scoped questions (e.g., 'What is 2+2?', 'What color is the sky?') and verify the response is on-topic. For robustness, run multiple questions and either use human review or a secondary scoring mechanism to confirm relevance. Verify that the response contains relevant terms and directly addresses the question asked.

behaviors.description: The tone re-check correctly identifies when a response is too formal for a casual-tier customer and triggers regeneration
behaviors.justification: This behavior requires the tone classifier to work correctly (identify formal vs. casual), which in a modern chatbot is typically LLM-based or a trained ML model—both non-deterministic. Mocking the tone classifier hides the real failure: the classifier itself misclassifying the tone. To test 'correctly identifies,' you need the real classifier to run on real generated text. Without it, you're only testing the conditional logic ('if formal, regenerate'), not whether the identification is actually correct.
behaviors.test_description: Call the real LLM to generate a formal response to a casual-tier customer (e.g., by instructing it to use formal language). Pass this response to the real tone classifier. Verify the classifier marks it as 'formal.' Confirm the system triggers regeneration by calling the LLM again with a casual-tone instruction. Verify the regenerated response is marked as 'casual' by the real classifier.

behaviors.description: The full exchange (user message, generated response, any regenerations) is persisted to the database with correct timestamps
behaviors.justification: Mocking the database hides real persistence failures: schema mismatches, timestamp timezone bugs, constraint violations, transaction rollback, or replication lag. The failure boundary is whether data *actually* persists in a real database and remains queryable—a mock always succeeds. You need a real test database (in-memory, SQLite, or test Mongo instance) to catch these issues.
behaviors.test_description: Execute the full chatbot flow (with real LLM or mocked LLM, but real test database). Persist the conversation to a real test DB. Query the database directly to verify: all fields (user message, responses, regenerations) are present; timestamps are in correct chronological order; and timezone information is correct. Include at least one test case with regeneration to verify both versions are not stored or that the old version is correctly marked as superseded.

behaviors.description: When the tone check triggers regeneration, the second response replaces the first — the customer never sees the rejected draft
behaviors.justification: This behavior requires a real first LLM call to generate a response that triggers tone check, a real tone classifier to decide regeneration is needed, and a real second LLM call to verify regeneration produces a different response. Mocking any of these hides the failure: the system might skip regeneration, or regenerate with the same content, or return the wrong version to the user. The interaction between real LLM outputs and the tone classifier is non-deterministic, so you need the actual models to test the full workflow.
behaviors.test_description: Call the real LLM to generate a formal response to a casual-tier customer. Verify the tone classifier marks it as 'formal.' Confirm the system regenerates with a casual-tone prompt and receives a different response. Verify that only the regenerated (casual) response is returned to the customer and that only the final response is persisted (or the first is marked as rejected). Test at least one case where the first response is rejected and one where it passes the tone check.
